description: Unique identifier for each marketplace seller
Display name of the seller account
Date the seller account was created; young accounts with high activity are a fraud signal
Seller registered country code
1 if the seller is an authorised reseller of any protected brand; unauthorised sellers of protected goods raise grey market signals
When the row was loaded into the database
Unique identifier for each brand
Official brand spelling; raw variants like NIKE and nike are standardised to this during cleaning
1 if the brand is on the protection watchlist monitored by the pipeline
Lower bound of the genuine retail price range; prices far below this drive the price deviation signal
Upper bound of the genuine retail price range
Unique identifier for each marketplace listing
Seller who posted the listing; foreign key to sellers
Brand claimed by the listing; foreign key to brands; empty for unbranded items
Listing headline; screened for disguised brand terms like N1ke
Listing body text; identical descriptions across sellers indicate copied listings; missing descriptions are flagged not rejected because absence is itself a signal
Product category such as Trainers or Handbags; brand and category mismatches raise signals
Listing price standardised to GBP during cleaning
Always GBP after cleaning; conversions are counted in the quality report
Date the listing went live
Number of reviews on the listing; counts inconsistent with seller age indicate review fraud
Average review rating; distributions skewed to 4.9 and above are a signal
Image identifier; the same reference across unrelated sellers marks shared photos and supply rings
When the row was loaded into the database
Unique identifier for each review
Listing the review belongs to; foreign key to listings
Star rating from 1 to 5
Review body; near duplicate template text across reviews indicates purchased reviews
Date the review was posted; bursts in time indicate review farming
Unique identifier for each detection signal
Listing the signal fired on; foreign key to listings
Machine readable signal name such as price_deviation or brand_evasion
Which detector produced it: rule then anomaly then review then trademark then similarity then network
The measured number behind the signal such as the price to floor ratio
Signal weight from 1 to 5 used in composite risk scoring
When the signal was detected
Unique identifier for each alert
Listing the alert concerns; foreign key to listings
Seller the alert concerns; foreign key to sellers
Weighted composite score built from contributing signals
low or medium or high; drives triage priority
Workflow state: new then in_review then escalated then closed
When the alert was raised
